function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
When evaluating future orbital AI compute satellites, which disclosed accounting policies or asset categories provide the closest precedent for treatment? Does the filing explicitly resolve whether such assets would be capitalized or expensed?
I'll start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieve relevant accounting policy sections.
steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about accounting policies or asset categories in the SpaceX S-1 filing that could serve as precedent for the treatment of future "orbital AI compute satellites." I need to examine the filing for relevant accounting policies related to:

1. Capitalization of satellite assets
2. Property, plant, and equipment policies
3. Any specific treatment of spacecraft/satellites
4. R&D expense policies
5. Any discussion of orbital infrastructure or compute assets

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieve relevant sections.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.message: Now let me retrieve the key accounting policy sections related to property, satellites, and R&D:
steps.reasoning_content:
Good, the filing is loaded. Now let me retrieve several relevant sections:

1. Accounting policies related to property, plant, and equipment (which would include satellites)
2. Accounting policies related to satellites or spacecraft
3. Any R&D capitalization policies
4. Any mention of orbital compute or AI satellites

arguments.prompt: What are the accounting policies for property, plant, and equipment, including satellites, launch vehicles, and spacecraft in {{spacex_s1}}? Retrieve the full PP&E capitalization policy section.
What are the accounting policies for research and development costs, including any R&D capitalization or expense policies in {{spacex_s1}}?

Retrieve any disclosure about orbital compute satellites, AI compute satellites, Starlink satellite asset treatment, satellite constellations, and how satellites are capitalized or depreciated in {{spacex_s1}}.

results.content:
Based on the provided text from the Significant Accounting Policies and related notes, here is a comprehensive overview of the accounting policies for Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E), including specialized aerospace assets.

### **1. General Capitalization & Valuation**
*   **Cost Basis:** PP&E is stated at cost, less accumulated depreciation.
*   **Capitalization Criteria:** Costs to acquire, construct, or improve assets are capitalized. This includes parts, labor, and allocated overhead.
*   **Maintenance vs. Capitalization:** Routine maintenance and repairs are expensed as incurred. Expenditures that increase an asset's functionality, output, or expected useful life are capitalized and depreciated over the revised life.
*   **Interest Capitalization:** Interest costs are capitalized during the construction period for significant long-term projects (e.g., AI data centers). Capitalization begins when activities to prepare the asset commence and ceases when the asset is substantially ready for use. The capitalized interest is included in the asset's historical cost.

### **2. Depreciation Methods & Useful Lives**
*   **General Method:** Depreciation is computed primarily using the **straight-line method** over the estimated useful lives of the assets.
*   **Exception for Flight Vehicles:** Flight vehicle hardware (boosters, fairings, capsules) and spacecraft are depreciated based on the **expected number of average flights** (a units-of-production method).
*   **Satellites:** Lives are based on engineering studies considering on-orbit performance, propellant life, and technology evolution.
    *   Broadband satellites: **5-year** useful life.
    *   First-generation mobile satellites: **3-year** useful life.
*   **Leasehold Improvements:** Depreciated over the **shorter of** their estimated useful life or the related lease term.
*   **Useful Life Schedule:**

| Classification | Estimated Useful Life |
| :--- | :--- |
| Servers and networking equipment | 5 - 6 years |
| Satellites | 3 - 5 years |
| Machinery and equipment | 3 - 10 years |
| Flight vehicle hardware | 5 - 25 flights |
| Data center infrastructure | 20 - 25 years |
| Launch sites | 7 - 20 years |
| Buildings and improvements | 30 years |
| Leasehold improvements | Shorter of 7-20 years or lease term |

### **3. Specialized Asset Policies**
*   **Launch Vehicles (Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Dragon):**
    *   **Reusable Components** (boosters, fairings, Merlin engines, Dragon capsules): Classified as PP&E and depreciated over expected flights.
    *   **Expendable Components** (second stages): Treated as **inventory** until launched, at which point cost is recognized in Cost of Revenue.
*   **Starship:** Currently in development. A majority of costs (test flight hardware, Raptor engines) are **expensed as Research & Development**. Upon commercialization, costs will be capitalized as PP&E and depreciated.
*   **Satellites:** Capitalized costs include construction (parts, labor, overhead) and allocated launch costs incurred by the Space segment.
*   **Construction-in-Progress:** Comprises ongoing construction for facilities, equipment, and AI infrastructure not yet placed in service.

### **4. Impairment Policy**
*   **Trigger:** Assets are reviewed for impairment whenever events or circumstances indicate the carrying value may not be recoverable.
*   **Level of Assessment:** Evaluated at the lowest level for which identifiable cash flows are largely independent of other assets (the "asset group").
*   **Recognition:** If estimated future undiscounted cash flows are less than the carrying value, an impairment charge is recorded for the amount by which the carrying value exceeds fair value.
*   **Presentation:** Routine disposals are typically charged to Cost of Revenue or SG&A. Impairments considered outside normal operations are presented in the "Impairment" line item.

### **5. Accounting for Flight Vehicle Costs**
*   **Cost of Revenue:** Includes the cost of second stages (inventory), depreciation of reusable components, launch operations, and related overhead.
*   **Useful Life Estimation for Flight Vehicles:** Based on three key criteria:
    1.  Ability to successfully recover and refurbish for reuse.
    2.  Economic feasibility of additional flights (supported by refurbishment cost analyses).
    3.  Customer acceptance for reflown hardware.

### **6. Key Disclosures & Recent Activity**
*   **PP&E Growth:** The company has seen significant capital expenditure growth, particularly in servers, satellites, data center infrastructure, and construction-in-progress for AI and expansion projects.
*   **Capitalized Interest:** Interest was capitalized for major projects like AI data centers (e.g., $169 million in 2025).
*   **Impairment Charges:** Charges have been recorded for damaged flight vehicles, abandoned production lines, damaged satellite hardware, and facilities consolidation, depending on the segment and nature of the event.

---
**Summary:** The company's PP&E policies reflect its hybrid aerospace and technology operations. Key features include the use of **straight-line depreciation** for most assets, with a **units-of-flight depreciation method** for reusable launch vehicles, and specific capitalization rules for satellites and major construction projects. The policies require significant management judgment in estimating useful lives, expected flights, and impairment triggers.

Based on the provided text from the Significant Accounting Policies note and related MD&A sections, here is a summary of the accounting policies for research and development (R&D) costs:

### General R&D Expense Policy
The company expenses R&D costs as incurred. These expenses include:
*   Materials, employee compensation (salaries, benefits, share-based compensation), contractor costs.
*   Cloud computing, data services, equipment leases, depreciation of R&D equipment, and allocated overhead.
*   Certain expenses for features/modules developed through engineering services where the company retains the IP.

### Software Development Costs
*   Software development costs marketed under on-premise perpetual license agreements are expensed.
*   Costs incurred prior to technological feasibility are expensed as R&D.
*   **Key Policy Point:** Technological feasibility typically occurs shortly before general release. Therefore, **all software development costs for the years 2023, 2024, and 2025 were expensed as incurred** (i.e., no software costs were capitalized during these periods).

### Segment-Specific R&D Treatments

**1.  Space Segment (Starship):**
*   R&D expenses primarily relate to Starship development, build, and testing. These costs (including test hardware, Raptor engines, compensation, tooling, etc.) are **expensed as incurred** during the current development stage.
*   **Anticipated Future Change:** Once Starship is commercialized (delivering payload to orbit), its costs are generally expected to be **capitalized and then depreciated in cost of revenue**.

**2.  Connectivity Segment (Satellites, Starlink):**
*   R&D expenses relate to next-generation satellites, Starlink Kits, and ground infrastructure.
*   **Capitalization Policy:** The cost to build satellites and capitalized launch costs are recorded as **Property, Plant & Equipment** and depreciated. However, the associated **R&D costs for their development are expensed**.

**3.  AI Segment:**
*   R&D expenses relate to training Grok models, developing next-gen AI products, and associated data center costs (including cloud computing, compensation, and depreciation of GPU hardware).
*   These costs are **expensed as incurred**.

### Capitalization of Assets (Related to R&D Output)
While R&D is expensed, assets resulting from successful R&D are capitalized under specific policies:
*   **Property, Plant & Equipment (PP&E):** Capitalized at cost less depreciation. This includes reusable spacecraft (Falcon boosters, Dragon), satellites, and general equipment. Depreciation methods are straight-line or based on expected flights for flight vehicles.
*   **Satellites:** Include building costs (parts, labor, overhead) and capitalized launch costs.
*   **Interest Capitalization:** Interest costs associated with significant construction of PP&E is capitalized from the start of construction until the asset is substantially ready for use.

### Summary of R&D Capitalization vs. Expense
| **R&D Activity / Asset** | **Accounting Treatment** | **Key Condition / Note** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **General R&D** | Expensed as incurred | Policy states all R&D is expensed. |
| **Software Development** | Expensed as incurred | Technological feasibility occurs just before release, leading to no capitalization in recent years. |
| **Starship (Space)** | Expensed as incurred | Currently in development stage. **Will shift to capitalization upon commercialization.** |
| **Satellite & Kit Development** | Expensed as incurred | R&D for development is expensed. The manufactured satellites are capitalized as PP&E. |
| **Grok/AI Model Training** | Expensed as incurred | Includes cloud computing, compensation, and GPU depreciation. |
| **Capitalizable PP&E & Satellites** | Capitalized & Depreciated | Successful R&D outputs (e.g., flight vehicles, satellites) are capitalized once ready for use. |

**In essence, the company's current policy is to expense virtually all R&D costs as incurred. The most notable exception anticipated in the disclosures is the future capitalization of Starship costs once the vehicle moves from the development stage to commercial service.**

# Disclosures on Orbital AI Compute Satellites, Starlink Satellite Asset Treatment, and Satellite Capitalization/Depreciation

Below is a consolidated retrieval of the relevant disclosures from the provided document chunks, organized by topic.

---

## 1. Orbital AI Compute Satellites

### Strategic Vision and Deployment Timeline
The company plans to deploy **orbital AI compute satellites** designed to handle energy-intensive AI workloads (such as inference demand) in **Sun-synchronous orbit** at far greater scale and efficiency than terrestrial alternatives. Starlink will provide low-latency, global connectivity linking these orbital AI systems to end users. **Deployment is expected as early as 2028.**

> *"satellites in Sun-synchronous orbit will be able to handle energy-intensive AI workloads, such as inference demand, at far greater scale and efficiency than terrestrial alternatives, with Starlink providing low-latency, global connectivity linking these orbital AI systems to people around the world and delivering real-time intelligence. We expect to begin deploying our orbital AI compute satellites as early as 2028."*

### Technical Design
The AI compute satellites are expected to **leverage the already-designed V3 satellite platform**, with substantially more power generated through significantly larger solar arrays — approximately **five times the solar array output** compared to V3 satellite designs. They are targeted to generate approximately **100 kW of compute power per ton**. No significant on-orbit assembly is currently contemplated.

### Competitive Positioning
The company states it believes it is **"the only company with a commercially viable path to building orbital AI compute at scale,"** underpinned by its ability to:
- Launch substantial mass into orbit cost-efficiently through **reusable rockets** (Starship)
- Manufacture secure, reliable, high-performance satellites at **low cost and high volume**
- Deploy a **modular shell approach** built around its scalable satellite constellation

> *"We plan to develop orbital data centers to enable scaling of compute capacity for us and our customers that is independent of terrestrial power infrastructure constraints. Space offers the potential to access virtually limitless power and an operating environment that supports sustained high-density compute, including structural advantages for power generation, cooling, and uninterrupted operations as capacity grows."*

### Operational Strategy for AI Compute Satellites
The company plans to build on its experience operating ~9,600 Starlink satellites in LEO, including:
- **Automated collision avoidance** (over 1,000 maneuvers/day in 2025)
- **Thermal management**: solar arrays facing the sun for constant power; radiator panels facing deep space for radiative cooling
- **Lifecycle management**: shifting older hardware to lower-intensity workloads, retiring systems through controlled end-of-life disposition (including graveyard orbits)
- Retirements **"may occur sooner than our estimates for the useful lives of our satellites"**

### Risk Factors
The forward-looking disclosures warn that:
- The deployment of orbital AI compute infrastructure is subject to **significant technical complexity, capital requirements, new innovations, and regulatory approvals**
- These investments may result in **"periods of reduced profitability or increased losses"** as the company prioritizes long-term growth

### Use of IPO Proceeds
Net proceeds are intended to fund:
- Expansion of **AI compute infrastructure**
- Enhancements to **launch infrastructure and launch vehicles**
- Increases in the **scale and capacity of satellite constellations**
- General corporate purposes

### Capital Expenditure Expectations
The company discloses that it has incurred and expects to **substantially increase** capital expenditures for satellite constellations, launch vehicles, ground stations, manufacturing facilities, **AI compute infrastructure, data centers**, and other supporting infrastructure. This includes costs for obtaining third-party GPUs, manufacturing its own GPUs, and constructing/leasing/maintaining data centers.

### Inter-Segment Launch Capacity Allocation
The company allocates significant launch capacity to its **Connectivity segment** for Starlink satellite deployment, and **"expect[s] to allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future."** These internal launches do not generate inter-segment revenue; instead, launch costs are capitalized.

---

## 2. Starlink Satellite Asset Treatment

### Capitalization of Starlink Satellite Launches
For launches of Starlink satellites, the company **does not recognize any inter-segment revenue**. Instead, launch costs are **capitalized in satellites** within **"Property, plant, and equipment, net."**

> *"For launches of our Starlink satellites, the Company does not recognize any inter-segment revenue, rather those launch costs are capitalized in satellites in Property, plant, and equipment, net."*

### Inter-Segment Cost Flow
When the Space segment dedicates launch capacity to deploying Starlink satellites, the associated costs are **capitalized within the Connectivity segment** and depreciated over time. No revenue is recognized in the Space segment for these launches.

> *"dedicated to deploying our Starlink satellites, we capitalize the associated costs within our Connectivity segment and depreciate them over time, and we do not recognize revenue for those launches in our Space segment."*

### Starlink Constellation Scale
As of March 31, 2026:
- ~**9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites** in Low-Earth Orbit
- ~**10.3 million Starlink Subscribers** across 164 countries
- ~**7.4 million monthly unique devices** for satellite-to-mobile services across ~30 countries
- Over **23,000 inter-satellite lasers** creating a dynamic mesh network in space

### Impairment of Starlink/Connectivity Assets
For the years ended December 31, 2025 and 2024, impairment charges of **$38 million** and **$63 million** respectively were recorded, related to write-offs of:
- Damaged flight vehicle in the **Space segment**
- Abandoned production line and **damaged satellite hardware in the Connectivity segment**

---

## 3. Satellite Capitalization and Depreciation Policies

### What Is Capitalized in Satellite Assets
Satellites include:
- **Costs to build the satellites** (parts, labor, and allocated overhead)
- **Capitalized launch costs** incurred by the Space segment to launch satellites to orbit, including **an allocation of the flight vehicle**

> *"Satellites include costs to build the satellites (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as capitalized launch costs incurred by the Space segment to launch the satellites to orbit, which include an allocation of the flight vehicle"*

### Interest Capitalization
The company capitalizes certain interest costs associated with significant acquisition or construction of qualifying assets (including **AI infrastructure data centers**):
- Capitalization begins once activities necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use have commenced
- Uses the **average accumulated expenditures** and a **capitalization rate derived from the weighted average borrowing rate**
- For the year ended December 31, 2025: **$169 million** of interest was capitalized (included in Construction-in-progress)
- **No interest was capitalized** during the years ended December 31, 2024 and 2023

### Depreciation Methods
- Most assets: **straight-line method** over estimated useful lives
- **Flight vehicles**: computed based on the **expected number of average flights** for each flight vehicle
- Leasehold improvements: depreciated over the **shorter of estimated useful lives or the related lease term**
- Management periodically reviews useful life estimates with engineering and operations teams

### Useful Lives of Satellite Assets
The company estimates useful lives based on:
- Engineering studies
- Historical on-orbit performance
- Propellant life
- Utilization patterns
- Design enhancements across generations
- Planned transitions to newer satellite technology

Specific useful lives:
| Asset Type | Estimated Useful Life |
|---|---|
| **Broadband satellites** | **5 years** |
| **First-generation mobile satellites** | **3 years** |

### Flight Vehicle Depreciation Criteria
Expected flights for flight vehicle hardware are based on three criteria:
1. Continued ability to successfully **recover and refurbish** the hardware
2. Continued **economic feasibility** of incremental flights, supported by declining refurbishment costs and sensitivity analyses
3. **Customer acceptance** for reflown hardware

### Maintenance vs. Capitalization
- **Maintenance and repairs** that do not extend asset lives → **expensed as incurred**
- **Significant refurbishment, renewals, and enhancements** that increase functionality, output, or expected life → **capitalized and depreciated ratably** over the identified useful life

### Depreciation Expense
For the years ended December 31:
| Year | Depreciation Expense |
|---|---|
| **2025** | **$5,915 million** |
| **2024** | **$2,977 million** |
| **2023** | *(not specified in provided text)* |

### Construction-in-Progress
Construction in progress is primarily comprised of ongoing construction and expansion of facilities and equipment, as well as **AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service**.

### Impairment Evaluation
The company evaluates impairment of Property, plant, and equipment at the **lowest level for which identifiable cash flows are largely independent** of the cash flows of other assets and liabilities.

### Reusable Flight Vehicle Classification
- **Boosters, fairings, and Merlin engines** → classified as **Property, plant, and equipment, net** (reusable)
- **Second stages** → classified as **inventory** (not reusable) until launched or assigned
- **Dragon capsule** → classified as **Property, plant, and equipment, net** (fully reusable)
- **Starship** → currently in development; a majority of Starship costs are **expensed to Research and development** as incurred

---

## 4. Satellite Constellations — Summary of Capital and Operational Scale

The company's capital expenditure strategy is deeply tied to its constellation deployment model:
- IPO proceeds are directed toward **increases in the scale and capacity of satellite constellations**
- The **Space segment funds R&D** that benefits Connectivity and AI segments: $930M (Q1 2026) and $3,004M (FY 2025)
- The **Space segment's financial results do not reflect the strategic value** it provides to Connectivity and AI through internal launches
- Deployment of 100 GW/year of orbital AI compute via satellites carrying **over 100 kW of compute per metric ton** will require **thousands of launches/year** and transport of approximately **one million metric tons to orbit annually**

steps.message: Let me also retrieve specific details on the AI segment's asset treatment and any explicit discussion of how orbital compute assets would be classified:
steps.reasoning_content: Excellent - I now have very detailed information. Let me also retrieve some additional specific information about the AI segment's accounting treatment and whether there are any explicit statements about how orbital AI compute satellites would be treated.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve the accounting treatment and asset classification for AI infrastructure, data centers, GPU hardware, and any orbital compute or compute satellites in the AI segment from {{spacex_s1}}. Include any discussion of construction-in-progress for AI assets.
Retrieve the segment descriptions and revenue categories for the Connectivity segment and AI segment from {{spacex_s1}}. How are satellite-related revenues and costs allocated between segments?

Retrieve any risk factors or forward-looking statements about the capital intensity, cost structure, or accounting challenges of deploying orbital AI compute satellites or orbital data centers from {{spacex_s1}}.

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the notes to the financial statements, here is a summary of the accounting treatment and asset classification for AI infrastructure, data centers, GPU hardware, and orbital/space-based compute assets.

### **Asset Classification**
These assets are classified within **Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E)** on the balance sheet. The specific line items used are:

1.  **Servers and networking equipment:** This category includes **GPU hardware** and other computing servers.
    *   *Useful Life:* 5 - 6 years.
    *   *Example Value (March 31, 2026):* $23,850 million.

2.  **Data center infrastructure:** This includes the buildings, power, cooling, and other facilities that house the servers.
    *   *Useful Life:* 20 - 25 years.
    *   *Example Value (March 31, 2026):* $2,965 million.

3.  **Satellites:** This category includes **orbital compute satellites** (referred to as "AI compute satellites" or "compute satellites" in the strategic disclosures).
    *   *Useful Life:* 3 - 5 years.
    *   *Example Value (March 31, 2026):* $12,893 million.

4.  **Construction-in-progress (CIP):** This is a significant line item used for AI infrastructure **not yet placed in service**.
    *   *Description:* The CIP balance is "primarily comprised of ongoing construction and expansion of the facilities and equipment as well as AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service."
    *   *Example Value (March 31, 2026):* $14,045 million (a substantial increase from $4,604 million at December 31, 2025, indicating massive ongoing investment).

### **Accounting Treatments**
1.  **Capitalization and Depreciation:**
    *   Costs are capitalized to the respective PP&E category once the asset is ready for its intended use.
    *   **Capitalized Interest:** Interest costs incurred during the construction of significant long-term projects, such as **AI infrastructure data centers**, are capitalized and added to the asset's cost in CIP. For example, $7 million was capitalized in Q1 2026. This capitalized interest is depreciated over the asset's useful life.
    *   **Depreciation:** Once an asset is placed in service, it is depreciated on a straight-line basis over its estimated useful life. Depreciation expense is recognized in the income statement, primarily within **Research and development** and **Cost of revenue** segments. For the AI segment, this includes depreciation of GPU hardware and data center assets.

2.  **Impairment:**
    *   The company evaluates PP&E for impairment at the lowest level for which identifiable cash flows are largely independent.
    *   **Space Segment:** Impairment charges have been recorded for damaged flight vehicles (e.g., $24 million in Q1 2025).
    *   **AI Segment:** Historically, impairment charges were recorded as part of **Restructuring charges** related to facilities consolidation (e.g., $36 million in 2024). More recently, AI segment Adjusted EBITDA declines are driven by heavy investment in compute infrastructure, but no specific PP&E impairment was noted for the AI segment in the most recent periods provided.

### **Strategic & Operational Context**
The financial notes confirm that the AI segment's strategy involves **extreme capital expenditure** in compute infrastructure. This is directly reflected in:
*   The rapidly growing **Servers and networking equipment** (GPU hardware) and **Construction-in-progress** balances.
*   The development and planned deployment of **orbital AI compute satellites**, which are classified as satellites but serve a dual-purpose for communications and AI compute.
*   The company's disclosure that it expects a "multi-year investment horizon" before its terrestrial and orbital data center deployments translate into sustained profitability for the AI segment.

In summary, the company capitalizes the costs of its AI infrastructure (GPUs, data centers, compute satellites) as PP&E, depreciates them over their useful lives, and capitalizes related interest costs during construction. The significant and growing CIP balance highlights the ongoing, large-scale investment in this segment.

Based on the provided text, the allocation of satellite-related revenues and costs between the segments follows a distinct strategic approach:

### **Connectivity Segment (Starlink)**
*   **Revenue:** Generates revenue through its **Consumer** and **Enterprise & Government** (including Starlink Mobile) subscriptions. For the three months ended March 31, 2026, this segment reported $3,257 million in revenue.
*   **Costs:** The costs associated with launching Starlink satellites are **capitalized within the Connectivity segment**. These costs are depreciated over time. Therefore, the Space segment does not recognize revenue or expense for these internal launches.

### **Space Segment**
*   **Revenue:** This segment's revenue is derived **solely from external customer launches and activities**, categorized as:
    *   **Launch Services:** Revenue recognized at point in time for customer payload launches.
    *   **Launch & Development:** Revenue recognized over time for government contract work.
    *   The segment **does not recognize revenue for launches dedicated to internal satellites** (e.g., Starlink). For example, in Q1 2026, its revenue was $619 million.
*   **Cost Allocation:** While it bears the R&D costs for programs like Starship ($930 million in Q1 2026), the direct launch costs for internal Connectivity or future AI satellite deployments are not allocated as expenses in this segment.

### **AI Segment**
*   **Future Allocation:** The text states the company "expect[s] to allocate a significant amount [of launch capacity] to our AI segment in the future." This suggests that as the AI segment's infrastructure scales, satellite launches for its purposes will be treated similarly to Connectivity—likely capitalized within the AI segment, with no corresponding revenue recorded in the Space segment.

### **Key Allocation Principle**
The central allocation rule is that **the Space segment is a strategic enabler whose financials only reflect commercial, external activities.** The substantial launch capacity used internally for the Connectivity segment (and in the future, for the AI segment) is treated as a capital investment within those respective segments. This results in the Space segment having "relatively lower revenue scale and revenue growth" compared to its actual operational output, as its foundational value is embedded in the growth of the other segments.

# Risk Factors & Forward-Looking Statements: Orbital AI Compute — Capital Intensity, Cost Structure & Accounting Challenges

## 1. Capital Intensity

**Extended Pre-Profitability Investment Horizon**
The company expects to incur "significant capital expenditures over a period of years before our AI products and services and other strategic initiatives, including AI compute infrastructure and in-orbit, lunar, and interplanetary industrialization efforts, become profitable, **which may never occur**." These expenditures span R&D, construction and expansion of production capabilities, acquisition of property and equipment, and ongoing maintenance and upgrades.

**Specific Capital Expenditure Drivers**
The prospectus identifies concrete areas of escalating spend:
- Design, development, and deployment of satellite constellations, launch vehicles, ground stations, and manufacturing facilities
- The **Terafab** initiative (a chip fabrication facility with Tesla and Intel)
- Obtaining third-party GPUs, manufacturing proprietary GPUs, and constructing/leasing/expanding data centers
- Development, testing, and deployment of **Starship** at scale, which the prospectus warns "will require the investment of significant additional capital resources"

**Acceleration Risk**
The company explicitly states it "may choose to increase or accelerate the pace of any of these investments at any time, which could result in **periods of reduced profitability or increased losses** as we prioritize long-term growth over near-term financial performance."

**Deployment Scale Requirements**
To deploy 100 gigawatts annually would require "thousands of launches per year and the transport of approximately one million metric tons to orbit annually." Even at a reduced 10-gigawatt deployment rate, the capital requirements for manufacturing and launch cadence remain material. The financial results reflect this pressure: the Space segment reported a **loss from operations of $(662) million** for Q1 2026 and $(657) million for full-year 2025, while funding **$930 million and $3,004 million** in R&D respectively.

**Subordination of Near-Term Financial Performance**
The prospectus warns that resources may need to be devoted "in excess of our current expectations, and there can be no assurance that these investments will generate adequate revenue, which could adversely affect our business, financial condition, results of operations, and future prospects."

---

## 2. Cost Structure

**Deployment/Construction Costs**
- **Launch costs** are the foundational variable: reusable Starship systems are expected to "significantly reduce the cost per kilogram to orbit," eventually approaching the cost of fuel alone. This is positioned as the key enabler — without Starship achieving its cost and cadence targets, orbital compute economics do not close.
- Terrestrial data center construction costs (building shells, MEP, grid interconnection) are "not applicable in space," which the company argues will make initial deployment costs of orbital compute "less than construction costs of others' terrestrial data centers" at full scale.
- **Satellite manufacturing** costs depend on achieving high-volume, low-cost production — the company claims its "advanced satellite manufacturing capabilities" give it an advantage, but this remains an assertion about a capability still under development.

**Processor/Hardware Costs**
- Moving compute to space "in and of itself" will **not** meaningfully reduce processor procurement costs.
- The hybrid sourcing strategy (internally manufactured chips via Terafab + third-party suppliers like NVIDIA) is intended to drive "the overall cost of our processors to decline" over time, while also alleviating "potential future chip shortages."
- **Intensive pre-deployment testing** is required to reduce chip failure rates in space, since the company does "not anticipate servicing or repairing processors in space." This represents a unique and non-recoverable cost factor — every failed chip in orbit is permanent capacity loss.

**Ongoing Operational Costs**
- **Energy**: Solar energy in orbit is characterized as "low cost and unlimited," creating a structural cost advantage over terrestrial data centers that depend on grid power.
- **Cooling**: The company expects to "leverage radiative cooling architectures, which incur no operating costs compared to liquid or air cooling."
- **Data routing**: The integrated Starlink network architecture "enables more cost efficient routing of data between compute clusters and to end users on a global basis."

**Structural Cost Disadvantage Risks**
- **In-orbit refueling** of Starship is "essential" for lunar, Mars, and deep-space ambitions and is described as "complex" with no prior demonstration — delays or failures would cascade into cost overruns across the entire orbital infrastructure roadmap.
- The company acknowledges that if unanticipated redesigns, additional testing, or operational setbacks occur, it "may experience delays and incur significant additional costs."

---

## 3. Accounting & Financial Reporting Challenges

**Capitalization vs. Expensing Uncertainty**
Given the early-stage, unproven nature of orbital AI compute (described as "in early stages of conception, design and development"), the timing and treatment of expenditures — whether capitalized as assets or expensed — introduces uncertainty. The disclosure that technologies "may not achieve commercial viability" raises questions about impairment risk for capitalized development costs.

**Impairment and Depreciation Risk**
Orbital AI compute infrastructure "will not be readily accessible, and as a result, will not be easily repaired or upgraded, such that any component failures could result in **permanent capacity loss, accelerated depreciation, decommissioning or need for replacement** of the infrastructure." This creates a unique accounting challenge: asset useful life is subject to space-environment hazards (radiation, micrometeoroids, thermal cycling) that "testing cannot fully replicate."

**Revenue Recognition Complexity**
The prospectus notes that several anticipated market opportunities "do not currently exist" and that "any estimate we make regarding the size or timing of our anticipated market opportunities is inherently uncertain." This affects forward revenue projections and the basis for deferred revenue or contract asset recognition under ASC 606.

**Segment Reporting**
The Space segment's financial results (losses of hundreds of millions per quarter, with heavy R&D spend) are disclosed separately, but the interdependencies between segments — particularly the shared Starship, satellite platform, and ground infrastructure costs — create allocation challenges. Adjusted EBITDA is presented as a key metric, but the reconciliation to GAAP figures would need to capture stock-based compensation, depreciation, and other non-cash items significant to a capital-intensive space infrastructure business.

**Contingent Liabilities and Insurance**
The growing challenges of space debris management may require "additional technologies or processes to safeguard our assets," and satellite damage from collisions could expose the company to "potential contractual liabilities or insurance claims." The accounting for self-insurance reserves or third-party insurance costs for orbital assets without established actuarial precedent presents novel challenges.

---

## 4. Competitive & Market Risk to Orbital Compute Economics

**Terrestrial Energy Breakthrough Risk**
The viability of orbital AI compute "depends in part on the cost advantages of solar energy relative to existing terrestrial energy sources." If breakthrough developments in terrestrial energy — specifically advances in **nuclear energy** — "significantly reduce energy costs or alleviate infrastructure constraints, the viability of our orbital AI compute infrastructure may be materially diminished."

**Demand Risk**
"Even if our orbital AI compute infrastructure proves to be commercially viable, a material slowdown in the growth of AI applications and related compute demand could result in existing terrestrial data centers sufficiently meeting such demand, thereby reducing the need for our orbital AI compute infrastructure."

**Starship Dependency**
The entire orbital AI compute strategy is contingent on Starship achieving its development, cost, and cadence targets. Delays or failures in the Starship program "would materially and adversely affect" the ability to deploy V3 satellites and orbital AI compute infrastructure. This single-point-of-failure dependency is the most significant structural risk to the capital intensity thesis.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have comprehensive information to answer this question. Let me synthesize everything into a thorough analytical response.

Key points to address:

1. **Closest existing accounting precedents** for orbital AI compute satellites:
   - Starlink satellites (capitalized as PP&E, 3-5 year useful life)
   - Terrestrial AI infrastructure (servers, data centers)
   - Flight vehicle hardware (reusable assets)
   - Starship (currently expensed as R&D, will capitalize upon commercialization)

2. **Whether the filing explicitly resolves the capitalize vs. expense question**:
   - The filing indicates orbital AI compute satellites are in "early stages of conception, design and development"
   - The general policy is to capitalize satellites as PP&E once they're ready for use
   - The filing draws a clear distinction between R&D (which gets expensed) and manufactured assets (which get capitalized), but it doesn't directly address how orbital AI compute satellites will be classified or when they'd shift from R&D to capitalized assets

arguments.final_result:
## Analysis: Accounting Precedent for Orbital AI Compute Satellites in the SpaceX S-1

### 1. Closest Precedent Asset Categories and Policies

The filing provides **four distinct accounting precedents** that are relevant to the treatment of future orbital AI compute satellites, listed in order of closeness:

---

#### A. Starlink Satellites (Closest Precedent — Capitalized as PP&E)

Starlink satellites are the most directly analogous asset. They are **capitalized as Property, Plant & Equipment** and classified under "Satellites" on the balance sheet. The capitalization policy is explicit:

- **What is capitalized:** Costs to build the satellites (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) **plus** capitalized launch costs incurred by the Space segment, including an allocation of the flight vehicle hardware.
- **Inter-segment launch treatment:** For launches of Starlink satellites, the Space segment **does not recognize any inter-segment revenue**; instead, launch costs are capitalized within the Connectivity segment's satellite asset balance.
- **Depreciation method:** Straight-line over estimated useful lives:
  - Broadband satellites: **5 years**
  - First-generation mobile satellites: **3 years**
- **Useful life determinants:** Engineering studies, on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, design enhancements, and planned technology transitions.
- **Balance as of March 31, 2026:** $12,893 million in satellite assets (all segments combined).

The filing also discloses that the company "expect[s] to allocate a significant amount [of launch capacity] to our AI segment in the future," indicating the inter-segment capitalization model used for Connectivity-segment satellites is the **intended template** for AI-segment satellites.

---

#### B. Terrestrial AI Infrastructure (Servers, Data Centers, CIP)

The AI segment already capitalizes substantial compute infrastructure:

- **Servers and networking equipment** (including GPU hardware): 5–6 year useful life, $23,850 million as of March 31, 2026.
- **Data center infrastructure** (buildings, power, cooling): 20–25 year useful life, $2,965 million.
- **Construction-in-progress:** $14,045 million as of March 31, 2026 — described as "primarily comprised of ongoing construction and expansion of the facilities and equipment as well as AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service."
- **Capitalized interest:** $169 million in 2025 and $7 million in Q1 2026 for AI data center construction.

This establishes that the company treats deployed compute hardware as **capital assets**, not operating expenses, regardless of whether the compute sits on the ground or in orbit.

---

#### C. Reusable Flight Vehicle Hardware (Units-of-Production Depreciation)

Reusable boosters, fairings, and Merlin engines are capitalized as PP&E and depreciated based on **expected number of average flights** (a units-of-production method), estimated at 5–25 flights. This precedent is relevant because:

- Orbital AI compute satellites will be launched on reusable Starship vehicles.
- The filing contemplates that satellites "may occur sooner than our estimates for the useful lives" and will be shifted to lower-intensity workloads before retirement — a lifecycle pattern analogous to flight vehicle reuse economics.

However, orbital AI compute satellites themselves are not reusable in the launch-vehicle sense; they are deployed assets that operate until decommissioned. The **satellite depreciation model (straight-line over estimated life)** is more applicable than the flight-vehicle model.

---

#### D. Starship (R&D-to-Capitalization Transition Precedent)

Starship provides the filing's clearest **transition precedent** from expensing to capitalization:

- **Current treatment:** A majority of Starship costs (test hardware, Raptor engines, tooling) are **expensed as R&D** because the vehicle is in development.
- **Anticipated treatment:** "Upon commercialization, costs will be capitalized as PP&E and depreciated."

This explicitly establishes management's framework: **development-stage assets are expensed; commercially deployed assets are capitalized.** This is the key precedent for understanding when orbital AI compute satellite expenditures would shift from R&D to capitalized PP&E.

---

### 2. Does the Filing Explicitly Resolve Whether Orbital AI Compute Satellites Would Be Capitalized or Expensed?

**The filing does NOT explicitly resolve this question for orbital AI compute satellites.** However, it provides strong, filing-supported inferences:

#### What the filing says explicitly:
- Orbital AI compute satellites are described as being "in early stages of conception, design and development."
- The deployment target is "as early as 2028."
- The general R&D policy is that all R&D costs are **expensed as incurred**, and this has been the consistent treatment for 2023–2025 (no software capitalization, Starship development expensed, next-generation satellite R&D expensed).
- The general PP&E policy is that **manufactured satellites are capitalized** once ready for intended use, with both construction costs and launch costs included in the asset basis.

#### Filing-supported inference — a two-phase treatment:

The filing supports the conclusion that orbital AI compute satellites would follow a **two-phase accounting treatment** consistent with existing policies:

| Phase | Treatment | Precedent |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1: Design, development, prototyping, testing** | Expensed as R&D | Starship treatment; general R&D policy; next-generation satellite R&D treatment |
| **Phase 2: Manufactured and deployed for commercial operation** | Capitalized as Satellites under PP&E; depreciated straight-line over estimated useful life (likely 3–5 years based on existing satellite useful lives) | Starlink satellite treatment; Starship commercialization transition |

#### Key unresolved questions the filing does not address:

1. **Useful life classification:** The filing lists satellite useful lives at 3–5 years (broadband = 5 years, first-gen mobile = 3 years). Orbital AI compute satellites have an unspecified useful life. The filing warns that retirements "may occur sooner than our estimates for the useful lives of our satellites" and that assets "will not be readily accessible" for repair, creating "permanent capacity loss, accelerated depreciation, decommissioning or need for replacement." The appropriate useful life estimate for a novel asset class is **not disclosed**.

2. **Cost allocation between segments:** The filing confirms the inter-segment capitalization model (Space segment launches → costs capitalized in the receiving segment) will apply to the AI segment. However, the **specific cost allocation methodology** for AI compute satellites — particularly how launch costs, manufacturing costs, and shared-platform costs (V3 satellite platform) will be bifurcated between Connectivity and AI segments — is **not explicitly detailed**.

3. **Revenue recognition for orbital compute services:** The filing acknowledges that anticipated market opportunities "do not currently exist" and that any estimate of size or timing "is inherently uncertain." Whether orbital AI compute revenue would follow a subscription model, usage-based model, or other structure is **not resolved**, which affects the impairment and recoverability analysis for capitalized assets.

4. **Impairment triggers unique to space-based assets:** The filing warns that orbital assets face hazards (radiation, micrometeoroids, thermal cycling) that "testing cannot fully replicate," and that component failures could result in "permanent capacity loss." The filing does not specify whether orbital AI compute assets would be assessed for impairment at the individual-satellite level, the shell/constellation level, or the asset-group level — though the general policy requires assessment at "the lowest level for which identifiable cash flows are largely independent."

---

### 3. Summary

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| **Closest accounting precedent?** | Starlink satellites — capitalized as PP&E ("Satellites"), including construction and launch costs, depreciated straight-line over 3–5 years |
| **Supporting precedent?** | Terrestrial AI compute infrastructure (servers, data centers) — capitalized with 5–6 year and 20–25 year useful lives |
| **Transition precedent?** | Starship — establishes the R&D-expense → PP&E-capitalization framework upon commercialization |
| **Does the filing explicitly resolve capitalize vs. expense?** | **No.** The filing does not contain a specific accounting policy statement for orbital AI compute satellites. |
| **Filing-supported inference?** | Development costs will be **expensed as R&D**; manufactured and deployed satellites will be **capitalized as PP&E** and depreciated — consistent with the Starlink satellite and Starship commercialization precedents. |
| **Key unresolved gap?** | Useful life estimate, inter-segment cost allocation methodology, and impairment framework for a novel asset class operating in a non-serviceable environment |

---

**Sources:**

| Source | Description |
|---|---|
| [SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026)](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm) | Primary source — Significant Accounting Policies (PP&E, R&D, Satellites, Impairment), Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements, Risk Factors, MD&A |

### 1. Closest Precedent Asset Categories and Policies

---

#### A. Starlink Satellites (Closest Precedent — Capitalized as PP&E)

The filing also discloses that the company "expect[s] to allocate a significant amount [of launch capacity] to our AI segment in the future," indicating the inter-segment capitalization model used for Connectivity-segment satellites is the **intended template** for AI-segment satellites.

#### Filing-supported inference — a two-phase treatment:

| Phase | Treatment | Precedent |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1: Design, development, prototyping, testing** | Expensed as R&D | Starship treatment; general R&D policy; next-generation satellite R&D treatment |

1. **Useful life classification:** The filing lists satellite useful lives at 3–5 years (broadband = 5 years, first-gen mobile = 3 years). Orbital AI compute satellites have an unspecified useful life. | Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| **Closest accounting precedent?** | Starlink satellites — capitalized as PP&E ("Satellites"), including construction and launch costs, depreciated straight-line over 3–5 years |
| **Supporting precedent?** | Terrestrial AI compute infrastructure (servers, data centers) — capitalized with 5–6 year and 20–25 year useful lives |
